summary: A Chinese dynasty that ruled from 618 to 907 CE, succeeding the short-lived Sui and generally regarded as a golden age of Chinese civilization, especially for poetry (Li Bai, Du Fu, Wang Wei). Founded by Emperor Gaozu and consolidated by his son Taizong, who won the Turkic title Tian Kehan. Its rule was interrupted by Empress Wu Zetian, the only woman to reign as Chinese emperor in her own right, who founded a rival Zhou dynasty. The long reign of Emperor Xuanzong, remembered for his consort Yang Guifei, gave way to the catastrophic An Lushan Rebellion, from which the dynasty never fully recovered. Ruled from the cosmopolitan capital Chang'an, it fought Tibet, Korea, and the Abbasid Caliphate (at the Battle of Talas), and was finally toppled after the Huang Chao Rebellion, giving way to the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the dynasty is identified in giveaways: a Chinese dynasty that ruled 618-907 CE, succeeding the Sui and eventually giving way to the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period (and later the Song). Ruled from the capital Chang'an and considered a, if not the, golden age of Chinese civilization, particularly for poetry. Its rule was interrupted by Empress Wu Zetian's Zhou dynasty and gravely weakened by the An Lushan Rebellion.

clues.clue: A Chinese dynasty that lasted from 618 to 907 CE, succeeding the Sui dynasty and eventually giving way to the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period
examples: name this Chinese dynasty whose second emperor was Taizong and which lasted from 618 to 907.
identify this Chinese dynasty that succeeded the Sui and ruled from 618 to 907 CE.

clues.clue: Its rule was interrupted by Empress Wu Zetian, who founded her own (Second/Southern) Zhou dynasty
examples: name this dynasty of China that was briefly ruled by Wu Zetian and faced the An Lushan Rebellion.
The rule of this dynasty was interrupted by Empress Wu Zetian.

clues.clue: Devastated/gravely weakened by the An Lushan Rebellion
examples: name this dynasty that faced the An Lushan rebellion and was interrupted by Wu Zetian after succeeding the Sui.
It was briefly interrupted by the rule of Empress Wu Zetian... and it was greatly weakened by the An Lushan rebellion.

clues.clue: Ruled China from the capital Chang'an
examples: Xuanzong ruled from Chang'an during what Chinese dynasty known for its poetry?
Name this 'golden age' Chinese dynasty led from Chang'an who quickly adopted an expansionist foreign policy towards its neighbors, similar to their predecessors in the Sui.

clues.clue: Regarded as a golden age of Chinese civilization, especially for its poetry
examples: name this dynasty preceded by the Sui, the cultural high point of Chinese civilization.
Name this longest ruling Chancellor... typically regarded as the first post-Han Golden Age in China.

works.name: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong
works.description: The dynasty was founded by Emperor Gaozu (Li Yuan), who rebelled against and defeated the last Sui emperor with help from his children, including his daughter Princess Pingyang's self-raised 'Army of the Lady.' Gaozu abdicated after his son Li Shimin seized power by killing his brothers in the Xuanwu Gate Incident; as Emperor Taizong, Li Shimin defeated the Eastern Turkic Khanate, won the title Tian Kehan, and sent the monk Xuanzang on a pilgrimage to India for Buddhist scriptures.

clues.clue: First ruler Gaozu (Li Yuan) rebelled against and defeated the last Sui emperor, aided by the 70,000-strong 'Army of the Lady,' raised by his daughter Princess Pingyang, who dispatched her non-eunuch servant Ma Sanbao to recruit rural bandits
examples: Its first ruler, Gaozu, defeated the last Sui emperor.
The 70,000 strong 'Army of the Lady' that supported this dynasty was raised and led by Princess Pingyang.

clues.clue: Its second ruler seized power in the Xuanwu Gate Incident, killing two brothers (including the Crown Prince) and forcing his father Gaozu to abdicate
examples: One ruler of this dynasty killed two of his brothers, including the Crown Prince, during the Xuanwu Gate Incident.
That emperor took power by killing his brothers at the Xuanwu Gate.

clues.clue: That second ruler, Taizong, won the title Tian Kehan ('Heavenly Khan') after defeating the Eastern Turkic Khanate
examples: That second ruler of this dynasty won the title Tian Kehan after defeating the Eastern Turkish Khanate.
An emperor of this dynasty took the title Tian Kehan after defeating Turkish nomads.

clues.clue: A commander became chancellor after capturing Illig Qaghan, ruler of the Eastern Turkic Khaganate; Bilge Khan's Orkhon inscription later decried the recruitment of Turkic officers into the dynasty's military after this defeat
examples: One commander from this dynasty became chancellor after capturing the ruler of the Eastern Turkic Khaganate, Illig Qaghan.
Bilge Khan's Orkhon inscription decries the recruitment of Turkic officers into this dynasty's military after it defeated the Eastern Turkic Khanate.

clues.clue: Taizong sent the monk Xuanzang on a 16-year journey from the capital Chang'an to India, where he studied at Nalanda monastery under the favor of King Harsha and brought back Buddhist scriptures, later housed in the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda
examples: A traveller from this dynasty spent time in the Nalanda monastery and gained the favor of King Harsha.
The Giant Wild Goose Pagoda was built during this dynasty and housed Buddhist scrolls that were brought back from India by Xuanzang for this dynasty's Emperor Taizong.

clues.clue: Co-founder Li Shimin (Taizong) was heavily influenced by his time on the frontier and brought many Turkic arts and fashions into China
examples: Name this dynasty whose co-founder Li Shimin was heavily influenced by his time on the frontier and brought many Turkic arts and fashions to China.

clues.clue: Taizong's niece (or daughter) Princess Wencheng married the Tibetan king Songtsen Gampo
examples: Songtsan Gampo married the devoutly Buddhist Princess Wencheng, a niece of the aforementioned second ruler of this dynasty.

works.name: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum
works.description: A former concubine of Taizong who rose to become the only woman to reign as Chinese emperor in her own right, Wu Zetian interrupted Tang rule with her own (Second/Southern) Zhou dynasty, legitimizing her rule through Buddhist prophecy. Her path to power and her family's court intrigues — including the poisoning of her son Zhongzong and the eventual restoration of Tang rule under Ruizong and Xuanzong — dominate the dynasty's early 8th-century history.

clues.clue: Declared herself emperor, interrupting Tang rule with her own (Second/Southern) Zhou dynasty, after her son Ruizong announced that his mother would be emperor
examples: This dynasty was interrupted by the second Zhou dynasty when Ruizong announced that his mother, Wu Zetian, would be emperor.
This dynasty was briefly interrupted by the self-proclaimed Second Zhou Dynasty.

clues.clue: Promulgated the Great Cloud Sutra, which predicted the return of the Maitreya Buddha, and had monks write a commentary declaring her the reincarnation of the Devi of Pure Radiance to legitimize her rule
examples: A ruler who interrupted this dynasty promulgated the Great Cloud Sutra, which predicted the return of the Maitreya Buddha.
A person who married into this dynasty proclaimed themselves to be the reincarnation of the Devi of Pure Radiance by having monks write a commentary on the Great Cloud Sutra.

clues.clue: A blank stele in front of the Qianling Mausoleum was meant to eulogize her; she had earlier ordered the death of Empress Wang and may have killed her own infant daughter to pin the blame on Wang
examples: A blank stele in front of the Qianling Mausoleum was intended to eulogize a ruler of this dynasty who ordered the death of Empress Wang.
A future empress of this dynasty possibly killed her own daughter to pin the blame on her rival.

clues.clue: Her son Emperor Zhongzong was likely poisoned via a pastry by his wife the future Empress Wei, in a conspiracy with Princess Anle; the plotters were overthrown in a coup led by Princess Taiping
examples: One ruler of this dynasty died when his wife, the future Empress Wei, poisoned his pastry, although Wu would be succeeded by the long-ruling Emperor Xuanzong.
This dynasty's ruler Zhongzong was likely poisoned in a conspiracy led by Empress Wei and Princess Anle, who themselves were overthrown in a coup led by Princess Taiping.

clues.clue: Ruizong returned to the throne with the help of his son Longji (the future Xuanzong) and his sister Taiping, who was later forced to commit suicide
examples: One member of this dynasty, Ruizong, returned to the throne with the help of his son Longji and his sister Taiping, who was later forced to commit suicide.

clues.clue: Regarded as the most powerful female ruler in Chinese history
examples: Wu Zetian, the most powerful female ruler in Chinese history, interrupted the rule of this dynasty with her own Zhou dynasty.

works.name: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei
works.description: The long reign of Emperor Xuanzong (Longji) is remembered as the dynasty's cultural high point, but also for his infatuation with his consort Yang Guifei, the last of the legendary Four Beauties of China, and for the corrupt, long-serving chancellor Li Linfu, whose appointment of non-Han generals like An Lushan to frontier commands helped set the stage for the dynasty's near-collapse.

clues.clue: Emperor Xuanzong was infatuated with his consort Yang Guifei, the last of the legendary Four Beauties of China, whose fuller-figured, round-faced look exemplified the era's beauty standards
examples: Emperor Xuanzong was infatuated with this one of the 'Four Beauties of China.'
This dynasty's beauty standards of a fuller figure and a round face were exemplified by the most recent of the Four Beauties, the consort Yang Guifei.

clues.clue: His chancellor Li Linfu, the longest-serving chancellor of Xuanzong's reign and an incredibly corrupt minister, deliberately appointed non-Han generals like An Lushan to jiedushi border posts to protect his own position
examples: This dynasty's chancellor Li Linfu deliberately appointed non-Han generals as jiedushi governors.
Name this long-serving Chancellor during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong. This incredibly corrupt and awful minister appointed non-Han Chinese like An Lushan to important border posts to protect his p…

clues.clue: A court favorite (Yang Guifei) had fresh lychees delivered by horseback from Guangdong
examples: A court favorite during this dynasty had fresh lychees delivered by horseback from Guangdong.

clues.clue: An emperor constructed China's first performing arts academy, the Pear Garden
examples: An emperor from this dynasty constructed their country's first performing arts academy, the Pear Garden.

clues.clue: One emperor established the Intrepid Militia
examples: One emperor of this dynasty established the Intrepid Militia.

works.name: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty
works.description: The dynasty's defining catastrophe: the half-Sogdian, half-Turkic general An Lushan, rumored to be the lover of Xuanzong's consort Yang Guifei, rebelled and founded a rival Yan dynasty, capturing the capitals Luoyang and Chang'an. Xuanzong fled toward Sichuan and was forced to have Yang Guifei executed; the rebellion (also called the An Shi Rebellion after An Lushan's successor Shi Chaoyi) was eventually suppressed, but it permanently weakened Tang authority and empowered regional military governors.

clues.clue: Sparked by An Lushan, a half-Sogdian, half-Turkic (Gokturk) general who was rumored to be the lover of Xuanzong's concubine Yang Guifei — who had even comically 'adopted' him as her infant 'son'
examples: This dynasty was crippled by a rebellion led by a Sogdian general who was the lover of Emperor Xuanzong's concubine Yang Guifei.
This dynasty was challenged by the rival Yan dynasty, founded by a man who once dressed up in a diaper and was 'adopted' by the imperial consort Yang Guifei.

works.name: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas
works.description: The dynasty fought near-constant wars along its frontiers: shifting alliances and warfare with the Tibetan Empire, three successive wars against the Korean kingdoms of Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla (allying with Silla against the other two), and westward expansion via the Protectorate General to Pacify the West, which collided with the Abbasid Caliphate at the 751 Battle of Talas — the dynasty's decisive defeat in Central Asia.

clues.clue: Tang forces under general Gao Xianzhi (Goseonji) were defeated by the Abbasid Caliphate at the Battle of Talas (751) after Karluk mercenaries defected, ending Tang control of Transoxiana
examples: name these medieval superpowers whose territorial ambitions over Central Asia clashed at the Battle of the Talas River.
A battle between these two empires was decided by the defection of Karluk mercenaries from the side of general Goseonji to the other.

clues.clue: The defeat at Talas is traditionally credited with spreading papermaking technology westward, after Chinese prisoners of war leaked the secret at a mill in Samarqand
examples: The first non-Chinese paper mill in the world sprung up in Samarqand after prisoners of war from one of these empires leaked the secret of papermaking to the other.
According to al-Tabari, a battle between these two empires led to the spread of paper-making to the west.

clues.clue: Established the Protectorate General to Pacify the West, with Four Garrison cities — Karashar, Kucha, Hotan, and Kashgar — in the Anxi Protectorate
examples: Karashar, Kucha, Hotan, and Kashgar were the Four Garrison cities this dynasty established in the Anxi Protectorate.
In order to control its western frontier, one of these empires established the Protectorate General to Pacify the West, which came into conflict with the other empire's eastward expansion.

clues.clue: Fought the Tibetan Empire in a near-constant series of wars and shifting alliances, losing much of its western holdings to Tibetan expansion; Tibetan troops briefly occupied Chang'an in 763 while Emperor Daizong was away

clues.clue: Emperor Taizong initially refused to recognize Silla's Queen Seondeok because she was a woman, but later allied with her
examples: Emperor Taizong of this Chinese dynasty initially refused to recognize Seondeok because she was a woman, but later formed an alliance with her.

clues.clue: The Nanzhao kingdom repeatedly switched sides between this dynasty and Tibet
examples: The Nanzhao kingdom repeatedly switched sides in conflicts between this dynasty and a western rival.

clues.clue: A shipwreck found off Belitung Island (in the Gelasa Strait) carrying luxury goods bound for the Abbasid Caliphate is the most complete evidence of maritime trade between the two empires, and the largest hoard of Tang artifacts ever found

works.name: Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, and Religious Persecution
works.description: The dynasty was a major center of Buddhist patronage — sponsoring pilgrimages and cave-temple complexes like Longmen — but also hosted foreign faiths including Nestorian Christianity, before Emperor Wuzong's Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution curtailed Buddhism's influence in China for centuries.

clues.clue: Emperor Wuzong's Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution also targeted Christianity and Manicheism as deemed forms of Buddhism; the Japanese monk Ennin was deported during it after lying about his origins and keeping a detailed travel diary
examples: The Japanese monk Ennin was deported during this dynasty's Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution.
This dynasty's emperor Wuzong included Christianity and Manicheism as forms of Buddhism in a Great Persecution that permanently reduced the religion's influence in China.

clues.clue: Built the Fengxiansi Cave at the Longmen Grottoes south of Luoyang, a site with 2,300 caves housing almost 110,000 Buddhist statues and over 60 stupas
examples: Name this site south of Luoyang, home to 2,300 caves that contain almost 110,000 Buddhist statues and more than 60 stupas.
This dynasty built the Fengxiansi Cave at Longmen Grottoes.

clues.clue: The Nestorian Christian monk Alopen, the first Christian missionary to reach China, arrived during this dynasty; his mission is recorded on the Xi'an Stele
examples: The first Christian missionary to reach China, Alopen, arrived during this dynasty.
The Xi'an Stele recorded that Christianity was introduced under this dynasty by the Nestorian missionary Alopen.

examples: This Tang official criticized the presence of the Buddha's finger bone in China in the essay 'Memorial on the Bone-relics of the Buddha.' This founder of Neo-Confucianism was renowned for his simple, …

clues.clue: A Buddhist savings bank stocked by donations, the 'Inexhaustible Treasury,' was liquidated by the state
examples: A Buddhist savings bank stocked by donations was liquidated during this dynasty and was called the 'Inexhaustible Treasury.'

works.name: Institutions and Economy
works.description: The dynasty administered its territory and population through inherited and new institutions: the equal-field land distribution system, the fubing militia, and eventually autonomous regional military governors (jiedushi) whose rise, especially after the An Lushan Rebellion, hastened Tang decline. Fiscal innovations included the Salt Industry Commission and a paper-currency precursor nicknamed 'flying cash.'

clues.clue: Regional military governors called jiedushi gained unprecedented autonomy, especially after the An Lushan Rebellion
examples: Powerful military governors called jiedushi during this dynasty gained unprecedented autonomy during it.
During the final years of this dynasty, regional governors called jiedushi gained power in provinces like Shuofang.

clues.clue: Required able-bodied peasants to serve in local militias under the fubing system, mobilizing for one month out of every five if living within 500 li of the capital
examples: Groups of peasants during this dynasty were conscripted into local militias that would mobilize during emergencies in the fubing system.
This dynasty was the first to require all able-bodied peasants to sign up for the fu-bing militia system.

clues.clue: Continued the equal-field land distribution system inherited via the Sui; it was the last dynasty to use the system, which collapsed alongside it amid the rise of autonomous fanzhen warlords
examples: This was the last dynasty to use the equal field system, and it was strained by a rebellion that called itself the Yan Dynasty.
The equal field system's reliable tax revenue led it to be adopted under the Sui and this dynasty after the Sui. The system collapsed along with this dynasty with the rise of fanzhen warlords.

clues.clue: Switched from copper coinage to a form of paper cheques nicknamed 'flying cash,' developed amid a coin shortage
examples: This dynasty marked a switch from copper coinage to a form of paper currency nicknamed 'flying cash.'
A cash coin shortage under this dynasty led to the development of 'flying cash' precursors to banknotes.

clues.clue: Created the Salt Industry Commission to sell merchants the right to buy government-monopolized salt, offsetting lost land-tax revenue
examples: This dynasty created the Salt Industry Commission to sell merchants the right to buy salt from a government monopoly.
This dynasty's Salt Commission enforced a state tax to make up for loss of land revenue from th…

clues.clue: Its institutions, including the equal-field system, were adopted wholesale by Japan's Taika Reforms
examples: The Taika reforms basically wholesale imported large parts of the culture of this contemporary Chinese dynasty, which was interrupted by the reign of Wu Zetian.

clues.clue: Korea's Goryeo dynasty later minted its first native coins in imitation of this dynasty's currency
examples: The Goryeo dynasty minted the first Korean coins after the style of this Chinese dynasty.

works.name: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei
works.description: The dynasty's poetry is central to its reputation as a cultural golden age, dominated by three contemporaries: the romantic, moon-loving Li Bai; Du Fu, the 'Saint of Poetry,' whose work chronicled the An Lushan Rebellion; and Wang Wei, who pioneered landscape (shanshui) painting alongside his poetry. Their work, and that of poets like Bo Juyi and Qian Qi, was later anthologized in the Three Hundred Tang Poems.

clues.clue: Li Bai and Du Fu (along with Wang Wei) were the era's most celebrated poets
examples: during which Chinese dynasty did poet Li Po live?
Wang Wei and Du Fu were poets from this Chinese dynasty, which was also when the moon-loving poet Li Bai lived.

clues.clue: Du Fu, the 'Saint of Poetry,' wrote 'Song of the Wagons' recounting his experiences of the An Lushan Rebellion, and is the most prolific poet anthologized in the era's poetry collection, Three Hundred Tang Poems, compiled by an 18th-century scholar nicknamed the 'retired master'
examples: The most prolific author in Three Hundred Tang Poems is this 'saint of poetry' who wrote about his experiences in the An Lushan Rebellion in 'The Song of the Wagons.'
Name this government. In the 18th century, a scholar nicknamed the 'retired master' compiled around three hundred poems from this time period into a much-reprinted anthology.

clues.clue: Li Bai wrote 'Quiet Night Thought,' describing the speaker's reaction to moonlight (memorized by Chinese schoolchildren), and other poems about drinking alone with his shadow and the moon
examples: Another Tang dynasty poet with this family name wrote the poem 'Quiet Night Thought,' which Chinese Saturday school students often memorize.
Li Bai also wrote about drinking alone with his shadow and this celestial body; in folklore, a jade rabbit accompanies th…

clues.clue: Wang Wei, author of 'Deer Park' (describing 'rays of sunlight' on 'green moss'), pioneered the impressionistic 'Southern School' of shanshui ('mountain-water') landscape painting
examples: Name this poem set on a 'lonely mountain' where the speaker sees 'Rays of sunlight' shining 'upon green moss.'
Name this Chinese dynasty whose painters included Wu Daozi and Wang Wei, the pioneer of shanshui painting. The impressionistic Southern School developed during this period.

clues.clue: Bo Juyi wrote 'The Song of Everlasting Regret' about the death of Yang Guifei
examples: Bo Juyi wrote the poem 'The Song of Everlasting Regret' after the death of Yang Guifei during this dynasty, one of China's Four Beauties.

clues.clue: Poems by authors like Qian Qi were later set to music by Gustav Mahler in Das Lied von der Erde, where the singer repeats the German word 'ewig'
examples: Poems from this dynasty by authors like Qian Qi were set to music in a piece that ends with the singer repeating the word 'ewig.'

works.name: Painting, Music, and Court Culture
works.description: Beyond poetry, the dynasty's court fostered painting, imported foreign music and instruments, and took up polo as an aristocratic (and sometimes deadly) pastime, while its distinctive sancai pottery and figurines survive as some of the era's best-known artifacts.

clues.clue: Painter Wu Daozi was active alongside Wang Wei; the Admonitions Scroll (traditionally attributed to Gu Kaizhi) has also been argued to date to this dynasty, whose painters developed the splashed-ink 'pomo' technique
examples: Name this Chinese dynasty whose painters included Wu Daozi and Wang Wei, the pioneer of shanshui painting.
The Admonitions scroll has been argued to have been created during this dynasty, which included an artist who developed the technique of 'pomo.'

clues.clue: A 'Library Cave' in Gansu province is a major surviving source of Tang-era painting
examples: A 'Library Cave' in this province is the source of much surviving Tang painting.

clues.clue: Nobles often used polo matches as opportunities to murder political rivals; Emperor Muzong died in a polo accident. Pottery figurines depicting women playing the recently introduced sport of polo survive from this dynasty
examples: Nobles of this Chinese dynasty often used polo matches as opportunities to murder their political rivals. This dynasty, whose emperor Muzong died in a polo accident, also faced the An Lushan rebellion.
Many artifacts from this dynasty are pottery figurines depicting women playing the recently-introduced sport of polo.

clues.clue: A style of ceramic glaze known in the West as 'egg and spinach' (sancai) was first introduced to the Middle East during this dynasty
examples: A style of ceramic glaze sometimes known in the West as 'egg and spinach' was first introduced to the Middle East during this dynasty.

clues.clue: The court's musical ensembles expanded from the Sui's seven to nine, adding a new classification called huyue ('foreign music'); poems like 'Bai Ge Xing' later inspired multi-movement Western compositions
examples: The seven ensembles located in the court of the Sui dynasty was expanded to nine during this dynasty. This dynasty added a new classification for a third type of music called huyue or foreign music.

clues.clue: Muslim traders first settled in China during this dynasty
examples: Its forces lost the Battle of Talas, and it was during this dynasty that Muslim traders first settled in China.

works.name: Decline and Fall
works.description: In its final century, the dynasty never fully recovered from the An Lushan Rebellion's damage to central authority. It was nearly toppled by the massive Huang Chao Rebellion before formally collapsing in 907, giving way to the fractured Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.

clues.clue: Nearly toppled by the failed civil-service examinee Huang Chao, whose rebel forces massacred thousands of foreign merchants in the port city of Guangzhou
examples: This dynasty was nearly toppled by the failed examinee Huang Chao.
Tang dynasty military expeditions into the Middle East led Huang Chao to massacre thousands of merchants in this city.

clues.clue: Women began fashioning their hair into the 'convict cinch' style in sympathy for the exiled Emperor Xizong, who fled to Chengdu
examples: Women began fashioning their hair into the 'convict cinch' in sympathy for an exiled ruler from this dynasty living in Chengdu named Xizong.

clues.clue: The dynasty's collapse in 907 was followed by the chaotic Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period, until the Song dynasty eventually reunified China
examples: The period following this dynasty is referred to as the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.
This dynasty followed the Sui and the Song eventually took over after its collapse.

clues.clue: After the dynasty's fall, Vietnam won its independence at the Battle of Bach Dang River
examples: After the fall of this dynasty, the Battle of Bach Dang River granted Vietnam its independence.

comprehensive_summary: The Tang Dynasty (618-907 CE) ruled China from the cosmopolitan capital Chang'an and is widely regarded as a golden age of Chinese civilization, succeeding the short-lived Sui and eventually giving way to the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period before the Song reunified the country. It was founded by Emperor Gaozu (Li Yuan), who overthrew the last Sui emperor with help from his daughter Princess Pingyang's self-raised 'Army of the Lady'; Gaozu abdicated after his son Li Shimin killed two brothers in the Xuanwu Gate Incident and took the throne as Emperor Taizong, who defeated the Eastern Turkic Khanate (winning the title Tian Kehan, 'Heavenly Khan') and sent the monk Xuanzang on a 16-year pilgrimage to India, via Nalanda monastery and King Harsha's court, to retrieve Buddhist scriptures later housed in the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda. A former Taizong concubine, Wu Zetian, rose to become the only woman to reign as Chinese emperor in her own right, interrupting Tang rule with her own Zhou dynasty and legitimizing her power via the Great Cloud Sutra's prophecy of the Maitreya Buddha's return; her reign was marked by the deaths of rivals including Empress Wang, the poisoning of her son Zhongzong by his wife Empress Wei, and the eventual restoration of Tang rule under Ruizong (aided by his son Longji and sister Taiping). Longji, crowned Emperor Xuanzong, presided over the Kaiyuan-era cultural high point but became infatuated with his consort Yang Guifei, the last of China's legendary Four Beauties, while his corrupt chancellor Li Linfu appointed non-Han generals like An Lushan to frontier commands. An Lushan, a half-Sogdian, half-Turkic general rumored to be Yang Guifei's lover, rebelled in 755, founding a rival Yan dynasty and capturing the capitals Luoyang and Chang'an; Xuanzong fled toward Sichuan and was forced to have Yang Guifei executed, while general Ge Shuhan's failed defense led to further losses before Emperor Suzong retook the capital with Uighur help. The rebellion (also called the An Shi Rebellion, ending with the suicide of An Lushan's successor Shi Chaoyi) permanently weakened central authority, empowering autonomous regional military governors called jiedushi and costing the dynasty territory that today forms Xinjiang. Abroad, the dynasty fought the Tibetan Empire in a long seesaw conflict (Tibetan troops briefly occupied Chang'an in 763), fought three wars against Korea's Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla (allying with Silla against the other two), and expanded west via the Protectorate General to Pacify the West and its Four Garrison cities, colliding with the Abbasid Caliphate at the 751 Battle of Talas, where the defection of Karluk mercenaries doomed general Gao Xianzhi's army and, by tradition, spread papermaking technology to the Islamic world via captured prisoners at Samarqand; a Belitung shipwreck laden with Tang goods bound for the Abbasid Caliphate remains the richest single hoard of Tang artifacts. Domestically, the dynasty administered its population through the equal-field land system and fubing militia (inherited from the Sui), later supplementing revenue with the Salt Industry Commission and a paper-cheque precursor called 'flying cash' as coin shortages grew; Buddhism flourished at sites like the Longmen Grottoes' Fengxiansi Cave, and Nestorian Christianity arrived with the missionary Alopen, but Emperor Wuzong's Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution later curtailed all these foreign faiths. The era's poetry — Li Bai's romantic verse, Du Fu's rebellion-scarred 'Saint of Poetry' output, and Wang Wei's landscape-painting-inflected lines — was later anthologized as Three Hundred Tang Poems and, in the case of poems by Qian Qi, set to music by Gustav Mahler in Das Lied von der Erde. In its final century, the dynasty never recovered from An Lushan's damage; the failed examinee Huang Chao's rebellion nearly toppled it outright before it formally collapsed in 907, ushering in the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period and, shortly after, Vietnamese independence at the Battle of Bach Dang River.
